text:
Microbes play a fundamental role in the global carbon cycle through the processing of soil organic matter (SOM), yet the specific molecular dynamics of this system remain largely opaque. Current theories suggest that a significant portion of SOM—particularly the dissolved organic matter (DOM) fraction—is comprised of low-molecular-weight compounds derived from microbial lysates and cellular metabolites. However, characterizing this small-molecule pool and determining how these compounds are partitioned between biological uptake and mineral adsorption remains a major analytical challenge.

In this study, we developed a streamlined untargeted metabolomics workflow and a novel 13C-stable isotope spike-recovery method to characterize the water-extractable organic matter (WEOM) fraction, which represents the most bioavailable component of SOM. We evaluated several mass spectrometry-compatible extraction buffers (including water, K2SO4, NH4HCO3, and various organic solvent mixtures) on both unfumigated and chloroform-fumigated soil samples. Using gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) and a library of authentic standards, we identified 55 primary metabolites. As expected, chloroform fumigation significantly expanded the diversity and concentration of detected metabolites, confirming its efficacy in releasing intracellular microbial contents.

To investigate the fate of these microbial products upon entering the soil matrix, we spiked soil samples with a 13C-labeled lysate from the soil bacterium *Pseudomonas stutzeri* RCH2. This approach allowed us to differentiate exogenous microbial inputs from endogenous soil chemicals with high precision. Our results indicate that the soil matrix acts as a significant sink; only 27% of the added microbial metabolites showed more than 50% recovery in the WEOM fraction. Notably, cationic compounds such as polyamines and most organic anions were rapidly sequestered, suggesting they are likely immobilized by mineral and clay surfaces. This study provides a robust framework for estimating the bioavailability of microbial metabolites and offers new tools for linking microbial metabolic pathways to broader terrestrial carbon cycling.
